speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 22, 1995. The survivor is Sam Zelikson. The interviewer is Sheila Scharfman. We are in Mount Kisco, New York in the United States of America. And we're speaking in English. My name is Sheila Scharfman, S-C-H-A-R-F-M-A-N. I am conducting an interview with Sam Zelikson, Z-E-L-I-K-S-ON. We're in Mount Kisco, New York. And it is August 22, 1995. And we are speaking in English. Can you tell us your name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Zelikson, Z-E-L-I-K-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the name you had at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sike.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-I-K-I-- E-- I'm sorry, E.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, did you have any other names during your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to-- my nickname, they used to call me [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: July the 1st, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Latvia, Wiskys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-I-S-K-Y-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mr. Zelikson, can you tell us about your life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we were born-- I had three brothers. Actually, we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] born in Latvia. My father had an iron business. We were going to cheder-- that's in Jewish to learn the Hebrew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bible. And my older brother, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, at that time, we were very small children, and I can't remember that much. But we were all going in a cheder and, of course, learning the, the Jewish religion, which was very important for us. We, we come from a very religious family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1940, the Russians came in. And they took the business from my father. And they said, you want to go to Siberia? All-- we need-- for a fireman. I'll give you a job as a fireman. And my father says, how can you give me a job there? I'm a businessman. So I already-- if you say one more word, I'll send you to Siberia. So my father says, OK, I'll become a fireman. And he become a fireman. At that time-- what were your brothers names also? I just want to know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Aba, ?] [? Izie-- ?] that's in English-- and Joseph. In Jewish was Abraham, Ichock, and Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, when the Russians came into Wiskys, did, did you all continue going to school? Was life--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the same as it had been before, in terms of your daily living?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, when the-- when the Russians came in, we moved from Wiskys to Dvinsk because my father's business became larger. And we had to move there-- bigger city. We couldn't-- he couldn't do it. So we moved to Dvinsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you have memories of Wiskys before you moved, what it was like as a, as a town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a small town. Of course we were going in cheder, in school. And we were saying the prayers. And if you said something wrong, the rabbi used to take a twig and hit you right over the, the fingers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a predominantly Jewish section, or was it mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there-- most was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- we had in Wiskys about 5,000 Jews. And the entire Wiskys was, maybe, population about 15,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that move specifically? Do you really remember the move from Wiskys to Dvinsk as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It was very exciting because my father and mother told us we were moving in a bigger city. And we'll meet more children. And we'll have more friends. Because I had some friends, but there wasn't that many children. And of course I was excited. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But when we moved there-- from Wiskys to Dvinsk-- and the Russians moved in, it was no more excitement. Because they took away-- we had a very big house. They took our house away. And they put their own officers. And they says it belongs to them. And they let us live downstairs. We couldn't use the house-- only in the basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers went to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school and what grades were you in? What were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time I was in public school. My brother was in junior high school. And my younger brother was also in public school. And my-- and the youngest brother was doing in Hebrew the cheder because he was too young to go yet in, in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In school-- you were describing to us how the Russians came, and they now forced to leave your house and live in the basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel those differences in school? Did you feel those differences in the town, generally? Was, was everything very different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was different. We didn't have the luxury what we had before. Everything was like a standstill. We couldn't do this. We couldn't do this. We couldn't say anything except to say in Russian because the Russians says, now you're a Russian. And you have to do what we tell you to do. And if you spoke-- like, my father used to have-- they have meetings and get together and spoke Jewish. And if a Russian came in-- he took away a couple of my father's friends because they spoke Jewish. They told them to speak Russian, so everybody knows what he's talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the, the school, they learned-- they taught us in the public school. Of course, we-- the main language was Latvian. But when the Russian came in, they say, you have to learn Russian. Because before, we didn't speak Russian. We only speak Lettish. That's from the Baltic State. But when they came in, they say, you have to learn Russian. So we start to learn Russian. And in the school, we had to do Russian. And at that time, I was in the sixth grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living with your family? Was it just--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, and my grandfather, and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the brothers, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your father actually get to open his business and have his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they took away the business. The Russians made him a fireman. Our business-- they took it away. And the entire business, they send it to Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They, they told him he's a capitalist. And in Russia, there's no capitalists. It's communism. And the children that went to school, we had to wear a red [NON-ENGLISH]. And they called us 'pioneers.'

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time were you still able to maintain your Jewish studies? Or did your family keep up the traditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunate-- you had to hide your Jewish study. We studied. But that was illegal. But we still went to cheder. We still read the Bible. And, of course, we went to synagogue Friday night, Saturday, and all the holidays. They didn't let us, but we did anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big and busy synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My grandfather, he was doing the candle-- the, the candelabras, the candles. He was a candle maker, and he was making for the synagogues. And he was, like, a [NON-ENGLISH] for the synagogue. It was a-- Dvinsk [NON-ENGLISH] is known in the world, the synagogue. The [NON-ENGLISH] have gone. There's my great-great-grandfather. He, he was-- started, actually, Dvinsk [NON-ENGLISH] the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even with the Russians there, the Jewish life in Dvinsk kind of kept going. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. Did you socialize and have non-Jewish friends as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any special activities as a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now, about 10 or 11?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time with the Russians, about at 11. Yeah, we used to play stickball. We used to play with the knife in the sand, hide and seek. You know, like children, they do all kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to run, catch. But at that time, there was no baseball. We, we used to take a long stick-- actually, break up a boom, take the stick, and make a small one-- and put two bricks and used to hit the small stick. And the farther you hit it, the better you're off. And on the other side, somebody had to catch it. And if you didn't catch, you had to run from one base to the other base.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's actually similar to baseball. But we used to call it 'stickball.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being aware of things happening in Europe, being aware of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the German changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When the German came in, that was 1941, right beginning of '41. I was in the street. And then I heard boom, boom, boom, boom. And I didn't know what it meant or what it is. And then I saw buildings were falling, literally falling, right on it-- on the floor. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a couple of friends say, oh, they're bombs, bombs coming. And then we saw a lot of airplanes. And they were flying low. And they kept on-- the bombs. And then I saw a soldier. And he, he pointed with a finger to me, come over here, Jude. And I, I didn't know what it meant, 'Jude.' He says, come over. And I didn't want to because I was actually afraid. I was 12-- not quite 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I start to run. He took the rifle, and he shot at me. But I was fast. And I ran the corner. And he missed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I come home. And I says to my father, a soldier shot at me. And they're bombing. There are a lot of bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, what are you talking about? What bombs? There's no bombs. I says, go outside. And you'll see, a lot of buildings went down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were living right by the water-- they call it the Dvina, the Dvinsk, Dvinsk Dvina. That's the biggest water in Dvinsk. We were living right near there. So of course he didn't see it. But I went far away, and I saw it. And then, as I was saying, a lot of solders came. And they say, all Jews, all Juden, get out from the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was-- they rode trucks. And they told us to go in the trucks. And I was separate-- my mother and me were separated. And my brothers and my father were separated. Because they didn't let us go together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the brothers in Dvinsk in the ghetto. The ghetto was an old-- the Russian soldiers used to use it-- barracks. And they took all, all of us into Dvinsk Ghetto. And they say to us, you want to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he pointed to me. I say, yeah, I want to work. And he says, come, do for me the-- light a fire. So I says to him, I don't know how to light a fire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took the boot, and he kicked me. And my father was right next to me, and he saw. He goes over to the soldier. And he says, why did you kick my son? He didn't do anything. He, he turned around. He took the rifle and shot my father in front of me. And was that a Russian or was that-- that was the German now?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was just as you were arriving at the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So he killed my father right in front of me. And I froze. I, I couldn't cry. I couldn't laugh. I couldn't do anything. I just went-- and since then, I can't cry. I went to specialist, the doctors. But they say they can do nothing about it because it's locked inside of me. And then they were looking-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the German officers, they were looking somebody to help and to clean-- it was a Jewish house. But they say it's their house. And me and my mother-- I-- so my mother and my brothers were separated, too. I says, me and my mother will go in and clean the house. They says, good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we cleaned the house for a little over a month. And we-- they closed the doors. And I said to my mother, I says, now we have a chance, maybe could run away. But wait a second-- I just want to go back, so I can understand something. You arrived at the ghetto. Your father was killed in front of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you then reunite with your brothers and live with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They separated-- my brothers was in one block. And I was in the other block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You're now living in barracks in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now you're with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were looking for our brothers. But the Germans-- the soldiers didn't let us to go to the other barracks. We had to stay in one block. And my brother were separate from them. And so I, I went, and we worked for the German officer. I worked for a month. And then I says my mother, now we have a good chance to escape, which we did. And we went back to Wiskys. I thought maybe in Wiskys, it'll be better. Maybe there's no Germans because it's always a very small city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Wiskys to Dvinsk is approximately about 20 miles. That's about 15 kilometer. And we went in Wiskys. And that was about-- that must have been around 8 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the market. And we saw a lot of soldiers. And I heard a lot of friends of mine, Jewish people, in the market. And I saw three trucks. And I says to my mother-- my mother said to me, something is wrong. Something is going to happen. And then we saw the truck open the canvas. And in there, we saw big guns. And the Nazi, the big officer, says something in German. And the guns start to fire on all the people. It was quite a few people. They start to fall. And my mother says, let's run away from there. But we didn't know where to go. And I says, you know, I, I know the-- I used to go with the, the farmer's kids-- we used to play. I says, there was a big farm. And the name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I'll never forget it as long as I live. And I said, we'll hide over there. But the door was closed. So I don't know how I did it. But somehow, you know, sometimes it's unexplainable. There was about four little nails. I took out the little nails, took out the glass, and opened the door. And we went in, in the farm in the straw, and we hide ourselves. I did it-- we did it for three days. And the third day, I took out, again, the nails, I took out the glass, and the glass fell out from my hand. And it broke. And I says to my mother, we're going to be in trouble because they'll find us. And before I said-- because we were deep under the hay-- I heard noises. And my mother laid down right on top of me. And they took pitchforks and started to dig in the hay to see if anybody's there. Luckily, the hay was too deep, so they couldn't do it. And they walked away. And they say, we'll get them next time. And my mother says, we better leave because they're going to find us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left at that-- the same night. And we started to go to Dvinsk because we didn't know where to go. And on the road, there was a, a Latvius policeman. He caught us. And they sent us back to Dvinsk Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to Dvinsk Ghetto. And then I started to work for the, the German again. And they let us go to see our brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where were you living when you went back? You were now sent back to the Dvinsk Ghetto. Was that a-- far way from the [NON-ENGLISH] farm? Did you travel a long way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- well, we traveled at least 10 miles before-- when we got caught. So they didn't send us back to Wiskys, they sent us back to Dvinsk. And at Dvinsk, they sent us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, when you were at the Dvinsk Ghetto again, you're with-- you're still with your mother? They let you stay with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they put you into a--

speaker: interviewee
text: To a different barracks than where we were before. The barracks where were our brothers-- my brothers were there and my uncles and aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew a lot of people in that barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then I saw the Germans go up. We were downstairs-- there was two flights. There was downstairs and upstairs. And I saw all the German soldiers going upstairs. And we couldn't figure out why. And then we find out all the women, they had their little babies. They took the little babies and opened the windows, threw out the babies in the-- through the windows. And the German soldiers were staying downstairs with bayonets. And they caught the children on the bayonets. And they had a lot of dogs, about 12 dogs-- German Shepherds, Dobermans. And they were feeding the dogs to the babies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feeding the babies to the dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the babies to the dogs, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you able to see that? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were downstairs. And we were watching when they were taking with the bayonets the children they threw out from the second floor. And downstairs, they were drinking. They were holding liquor in one hand and the rifle in the other hand and catching the babies and feeding them to the dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you were in this barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- I started to work again for the German. And I was with my mother and my three brothers. One of my brothers, the oldest one, got sick. So they separate-- they put him in a different barrack. And they ask whoever wants to work to work for the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I volunteered. And my mother went to work. And we saw a lot of freight trains coming in with clothes. We had to take out the, the, the-- [INAUDIBLE] the soldiers' clothes, empty the packets to find if there was wallets or money or papers and separate out. And when a lot of clothes came, there was missing a-- an arm, a leg. Fingers we find in the clothes. But that only went for about three months. And after the three months, they, they stopped. And me, me and my mother were separated again from my brothers. And we worked for the German. And we escaped again. We escaped again. And then we were going on a road back to Wiskys again. A Latvius woman started to scream, you dirty Jew, and picked up stones and started to throw stones. And she started to scream so loud, a lot of people came. And the [NON-ENGLISH] right away took us again. And instead of sending us to Dvinsk Ghetto, they send us to Dvinsk jail. They took us to Dvinsk jail. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Over there, I was separate from my mother. And they took me. And they say, did you see anybody-- did you see anybody has [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] guns or knives? Because you were hiding, do you know everybody? Do you know where the people are hiding? I told them, no. I was, at that time, what, 12 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- I'm going to show you. And he took a whip. And on the end was lead. And he hit me with the whip. And I still have a, a broken skull. And he saw that I didn't say nothing. And then I saw a wall. And it had mud on it. He says, come over, [GERMAN]. Stay by the wall. And he put me by the wall. And then I heard zzzt. And my whole body went-- like a shock. Then I find out there was electricity. And he did it for quite a few times. But I couldn't tell him anything I didn't know. Then he put toothpicks in my fingernails. And they put the-- a substance-- I don't know what kind it was. It was wet, the, the toothpicks. And he lit a match. And I screamed, but I couldn't tell him nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, you're a, a, a strong boy. You don't want to tell me. But you're going to tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
